planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix with support for climbing; Philodendron subhastatum is immediately distinguished from other philodendrons by the vivid brick-red to cherry-red undersurface of its leaves — a striking two-tone effect where the upper leaf surface is glossy dark green and the lower surface is bold red. This bicolored leaf quality is especially vivid on young, fresh leaves and fades to duller maroon as leaves mature. Best in warm, humid shade. Protect from temperatures below 55°F. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs — calcium oxalate in all plant parts.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil — Red-Backed Philodendron is one of the most ornamentally distinctive of the climbing philodendrons, valued primarily for the dramatic bicolored nature of its leaves: the upper surface glossy dark forest-green, the lower surface vivid brick-red to cherry-red. Philodendron subhastatum is a hemiepiphytic, climbing philodendron in the Araceae family native to Colombia and Panama — a member of the loosely defined informal group of bicolored-leaf philodendrons within the large and varied genus, sharing the red-undersided quality with species like P. erubescens and some P. gloriosum forms, though subhastatum has a more distinctly elongated, narrow leaf shape. The plant produces elongated, narrowly ovate to hastate (spear-shaped with spreading basal lobes) leaves 10–18 inches long on climbing stems that ascend a support using adventitious roots. The upper leaf surface is dark, semi-glossy green; the lower surface is a vivid, coppery brick-red to cherry-red that is especially striking on young, recently expanded leaves and remains ornamental through the leaf's life. When light comes through the leaf margin or shines on the red underside directly, the bicolor contrast is particularly vivid. In shaded garden settings, hanging leaves and side-light conditions regularly expose the red undersides to good effect. A distinctive collector philodendron with genuine ornamental quality for shaded tropical patios. CAUTION: toxic to pets.
sources: International Aroid Society — Philodendron subhastatum cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron subhastatum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental philodendrons for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: White Bat Flower (Large White Bat Flower, Tacca Lily, White Devil's Tongue)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with high humidity; Tacca integrifolia requires the same deeply shaded, warm, humid conditions as its relative the Black Bat Flower (T. chantrieri), but produces flowers in white to pale purple rather than black, making it arguably even more ethereal in appearance. The bat-like bracts (4–6 inches wide) spread out like wings above the central flowers and the long, thread-like floral filaments can reach 20–28 inches. Needs consistently warm temperatures above 60°F and high humidity (60%+). Difficult but rewarding. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with consistently warm temperatures and high humidity — White Bat Flower is one of the most unusual and spectacular of all shade-loving tropical perennials, producing otherworldly flowers unlike those of any other commonly grown plant. Tacca integrifolia is a rhizomatous, evergreen perennial in the Taccaceae family (now often placed in Dioscoreaceae) native to the tropical forests of Southeast Asia (Thailand, Malaysia, Borneo, India), a close relative of the Black Bat Flower (T. chantrieri) but producing flowers in white to pale lavender-purple rather than dark maroon-black. Both Tacca species are grown for the same extraordinary quality: the inflorescence, which consists of two large, wing-like bracts (modified leaves) 4–6 inches across spreading horizontally above the central cluster of small flowers like a bat's wings in flight, with long, thread-like appendages (filiform bracts) hanging 20–28 inches below — creating the impression of a ghostly bat hovering among the leaves. The overall color of T. integrifolia is white to pale green-white with occasional pale lavender or purple tints, giving it a more ethereal, moonlit quality compared to the dramatic darkness of T. chantrieri. Both the large bracts and the hanging filaments are characteristic of the genus. Tacca integrifolia grows from a shallow rhizome that spreads slowly in moist, humus-rich soil in deep shade. It produces large, glossy, undivided, broadly elliptic leaves 18–24 inches long that create an attractive foliage display year-round. Non-toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Tacca integrifolia species profile
American Horticultural Society — Tacca integrifolia: White Bat Flower cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Unusual shade tropicals for Florida zone 10-12
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Tacca (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Yellow Ginger Lily (Golden Butterfly Ginger, Yellow Garland Lily, Flavum Ginger)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Hedychium flavum is a vigorous, rhizomatous ginger lily that produces fragrant, clear yellow to pale-yellow flowers in dense, terminal spikes in summer and fall. More cold-hardy than many tropical gingers, it is root-hardy to zone 7 and recovers reliably from frost in zone 8-9 when mulched. The fragrance is sweet, intense, and jasmine-like, strongest in the evening. Excellent in moist, partially shaded positions near seating areas where the fragrance can be appreciated. Divide clumps every 2–3 years for best bloom. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Yellow Ginger Lily thrives in the warm, moist conditions of Gulf Coast summers and produces its fragrant yellow flower spikes most prolifically when given consistent moisture through the growing season. Hedychium flavum is a tall, rhizomatous, evergreen to deciduous perennial ginger in the Zingiberaceae family native to the Himalayas (Nepal, India, Bhutan, southern China) — one of the most cold-hardy and fragrant of the ornamental ginger lilies, known since Victorian times as a prized garden plant for the tropical-appearing foliage and the intensely perfumed, clear yellow flowers. The plant produces robust, cane-like stems 3–6 feet tall clothed in broad, glossy, bright-green, lance-shaped leaves 12–18 inches long — the overall effect lush and tropical. In summer and fall, the stem tips are crowned by dense, cylindrical, 6–10 inch flower spikes bearing 15–30 flowers. Each flower is a classic Hedychium bloom: a narrow yellow tube flares into a larger, reflexed, bright-yellow, three-petaled corolla with a single prominent stamen and style in the same clear yellow. The fragrance is the plant's outstanding quality — intensely sweet, rich, and jasmine-like, filling the surrounding air in the evening hours when it is strongest. This fragrance quality, combined with the cold hardiness (to zone 7 with mulching), makes H. flavum one of the most useful and rewarding of the ginger lilies for zone 8-9 Gulf Coast gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hedychium species for Florida gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ginger Lilies for Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens
LSU AgCenter — Hardy Gingers for Gulf Coast Louisiana landscapes
American Horticultural Society — Hedychium flavum: Yellow Ginger Lily cultivation

common_name: Antiquorum Taro (Imperial Taro, Small Taro, Dasheen)

planting.note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in partial shade to full sun in moist to wet, fertile soil; Colocasia antiquorum is an older botanical name for a group of small-corm taro types distinct from the large-corm Colocasia esculenta forms. In historical and botanical literature it denotes the classic 'small taro' with golf-ball to tennis-ball sized corms and a characteristic purple marking at the center of the corm. Corms are edible when thoroughly cooked. In zone 9, the plants die back in frost but the corms survive in the ground and resprout in spring. CAUTION: all raw Colocasia parts contain calcium oxalate and cause intense mouth irritation — always cook thoroughly.

directions: Plant corms in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained to wet soil — Antiquorum Taro grows vigorously in the warm, moist conditions of Gulf Coast zone 8b-12 and produces harvestable corms through a long growing season. Colocasia antiquorum is a tuberous, rhizomatous perennial aroid in the Araceae family — botanically, the name C. antiquorum has been applied to the smaller-corm types of taro grown throughout Asia, the Pacific, and the Caribbean that differ from the larger-corm 'dasheen' forms of C. esculenta in corm size, corm flesh color (often with a distinct purple center), and the number of cormels (small side corms). The plant is a core food staple throughout the Pacific Islands, the Caribbean, West Africa, and parts of Asia where taro is a traditional staple crop — the corms are peeled and cooked (boiled, steamed, baked, or fried) to neutralize the calcium oxalate crystals present in all raw Colocasia, and the cooked flesh has a distinctive mildly sweet, somewhat starchy, earthy flavor similar to potato. The large, distinctly peltate (shield-shaped, with the petiole attached in the center of the blade rather than at the edge), arrow-shaped leaves are ornamentally attractive and can reach 24–36 inches. In zone 8b-12, the plants grow through the warm months, die back to the corms in cold winters, and resprout in spring. The corms are harvested in fall before frost. Multiple smaller cormels form around the central corm and can be replanted. CAUTION: cook thoroughly before eating.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Colocasia (Taro) production in Florida
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Taro production in the southern United States
LSU AgCenter — Growing taro (dasheen) in Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Taro and edible aroids for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens

common_name: Five Fingers Vine (Arrow-Leaf Syngonium, Jamaica Arrowhead, Five-Lobed Syngonium)

planting.note: Grow in partial shade to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or as a container plant; Syngonium auritum is one of the native Florida species of Syngonium, found naturally in Collier and Monroe Counties as an epiphytic climber on trees in subtropical hammocks — making it distinct from the common houseplant Syngonium podophyllum (which is South American). The leaves are adult-form from an early age in favorable conditions, becoming large (12–18 inches) and distinctively divided into 3–5 spreading, finger-like lobes — very different from the simple arrowhead-shaped juvenile leaves of most Syngonium. Protect from frost in zone 9. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or as an epiphytic climber on a rough bark surface — Five Fingers Vine is one of the largest-leaved and most ornamentally striking of the Syngonium species in its adult form and is also notable for being the only Syngonium species native to Florida. Syngonium auritum is a climbing, hemiepiphytic perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to Jamaica, Cuba, the Caribbean, and southern Florida (Collier and Monroe Counties in hammock forests) — a member of the same genus as the common houseplant arrowhead vine (S. podophyllum) but considerably larger-leaved in mature form and with a distinctly different leaf shape. The plant climbs by adhesive aerial roots and, in juvenile form, produces simple, arrow-shaped leaves typical of Syngonium. As the plant matures and climbs higher, the leaves transition dramatically to the adult form: large (12–18 inches), deeply divided into 3–5 (occasionally more) spreading, broadly elliptic lobes, giving the leaf the appearance of a large, flattened hand with outstretched fingers — the 'five fingers' of the common name. The leaf surface is dark, lustrous, deep green. In southern Florida hammock forests, Syngonium auritum climbs naturally up tree trunks and over understory vegetation, its large adult leaves capturing light in the forest understory. A significant climber for shaded tropical patios and zone 10-12 outdoor landscapes. CAUTION: toxic to pets and irritating to skin from calcium oxalate.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Syngonium auritum: Florida native arrowhead vine
Florida Native Plant Society — Syngonium auritum species profile
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Syngonium auritum species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Syngonium (toxic to cats and dogs)
